questions.text: What is the primary prohibition established by the federal Anti-Kickback Statute (42 USC 1320a-7b)?
options: A. Offering or receiving remuneration in exchange for referrals of patients covered by federal healthcare programs
B. Referring patients to entities in which the physician has a financial ownership interest
C. Billing federal healthcare programs for services rendered by unlicensed medical personnel
D. Submitting duplicate claims to multiple insurance carriers for the same medical service
questions.justification_correct: The Anti-Kickback Statute (AKS) prohibits knowingly and willfully offering, paying, soliciting, or receiving any remuneration (including kickbacks, bribes, or rebates) to induce or reward referrals of items or services reimbursable by federal healthcare programs such as Medicare and Medicaid. The statute is intended to prevent financial incentives from distorting medical judgment and inflating program costs. Violations can result in criminal penalties (up to 5 years imprisonment and $25,000 fines per violation), civil monetary penalties, and exclusion from federal healthcare programs. Safe harbors exist for certain arrangements that meet specific criteria.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B describes violations of the Stark Law (physician self-referral statute), which is a separate civil statute addressing financial relationships and referrals. Option C relates to billing fraud involving scope-of-practice violations but is not the focus of the Anti-Kickback Statute. Option D describes duplicate billing fraud, which violates the False Claims Act but is not addressed by the Anti-Kickback Statute's prohibition on remuneration for referrals.

questions.text: What is the primary role of the Office of Inspector General (OIG) in healthcare fraud detection and prevention?
options: A. Conducting audits, investigations, and evaluations to detect and prevent fraud, waste, and abuse in HHS programs including Medicare and Medicaid
B. Reviewing and approving all new pharmaceutical drugs before they can be marketed to healthcare providers
C. Establishing reimbursement rates and fee schedules for all medical procedures covered by federal programs
D. Licensing and credentialing physicians and other healthcare professionals authorized to practice medicine
questions.justification_correct: The Office of Inspector General (OIG) of the Department of Health and Human Services is the primary federal agency responsible for detecting, investigating, and preventing fraud, waste, and abuse in HHS programs, including Medicare and Medicaid. The OIG conducts audits, evaluations, and investigations; issues compliance guidance and advisory opinions; maintains exclusion lists of sanctioned providers; negotiates Corporate Integrity Agreements (CIAs); and works with the Department of Justice on criminal and civil enforcement actions. The OIG publishes an annual Work Plan identifying priority risk areas and issues Special Fraud Alerts and Compliance Program Guidance to help providers avoid fraudulent practices.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B describes the role of the Food and Drug Administration (FDA), which reviews drug safety and efficacy for approval, not the OIG's fraud detection mission. Option C describes functions of the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS), which administers the programs and establishes payment methodologies, while OIG provides oversight. Option D describes state medical board functions and professional credentialing, which are separate from the OIG's federal oversight and investigative responsibilities for fraud prevention.

questions.text: Which of the following represents a common red flag indicating potential Medicare or Medicaid fraud?
options: A. Billing patterns showing an unusually high volume of services compared to peers or medical necessity standards
B. A healthcare provider requesting preauthorization from the insurer before performing elective procedures
C. Medical records documenting detailed patient history and physical examination findings
D. A physician referring patients to multiple specialists based on diagnosed medical conditions
questions.justification_correct: Unusually high billing volumes compared to peer providers or medical necessity standards is a significant red flag for potential fraud identified by OIG, CMS, and fraud detection algorithms. Outlier billing patterns may indicate upcoding, services not rendered, medically unnecessary services, or other fraudulent schemes. Other common red flags include duplicate billing, billing for services by excluded providers, unbundling, consistent use of high-complexity codes, patterns inconsistent with patient demographics or diagnoses, and billing for deceased patients. Data analytics, including predictive modeling and pattern analysis, help identify these anomalies for investigation. Legitimate high-volume practices can explain their patterns, but statistical outliers warrant scrutiny.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B describes proper preauthorization procedures, which demonstrate compliance with payer requirements and appropriate utilization management, the opposite of a fraud indicator. Option C describes thorough documentation supporting medical necessity, which is evidence of proper billing practices rather than fraud. Option D describes medically appropriate referral patterns based on patient needs; legitimate specialist referrals are proper medical practice and not indicative of fraud unless coupled with improper financial relationships violating Stark or Anti-Kickback laws.

questions.text: A CFE is investigating a cardiology practice where a pattern emerges: simple office visits (CPT 99213) are consistently billed as comprehensive examinations (CPT 99215) despite medical records showing minimal complexity. The practice's billing ratio for 99215 codes is 78% compared to a specialty average of 12%. What should the CFE do first to substantiate potential upcoding?
options: A. Compare documentation in patient medical records against the billing codes submitted to verify whether services match the complexity level billed
B. Interview the physicians immediately to determine their intent and knowledge of the billing discrepancies before reviewing any documentation
C. Calculate the total financial impact of overbilling and report the monetary loss directly to Medicare without further investigation
D. Subpoena all patient records and billing documents through legal counsel before conducting any preliminary analysis of the coding patterns
questions.justification_correct: In upcoding investigations, the CFE must establish that services billed do not match services documented. Comparing medical record documentation against submitted billing codes is the foundational step to prove the essential element of upcoding fraud: that higher-level codes were billed than what was actually performed or documented. This documentary evidence forms the basis for substantiating the fraud scheme under the False Claims Act and supports calculation of damages.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B is premature because interviewing subjects before securing and reviewing documentary evidence can compromise the investigation and alert subjects who may destroy evidence. Option C skips the critical substantiation phase; calculating damages without proving the underlying fraud through documentation comparison is procedurally flawed. Option D is unnecessarily aggressive and costly; subpoenas are generally not the first step when medical records and billing data may be obtained through routine audit procedures or administrative requests.

questions.text: A CFE discovers that a hospital laboratory director receives monthly payments of $5,000 from a diagnostic testing company, described in contracts as 'consulting fees.' The laboratory director refers all specialized blood work to this company, generating $1.2 million annually. The director provides no documented consulting services. Which element is most critical to establish an Anti-Kickback Statute violation?
options: A. Proving that one purpose of the payment was to induce or reward referrals of federal healthcare program business
B. Demonstrating that the payment amount exceeded fair market value for legitimate consulting services in all circumstances
C. Establishing that the laboratory director had direct decision-making authority over all aspects of patient care and treatment
D. Showing that the diagnostic testing company had previous enforcement actions or violations of healthcare regulations
questions.justification_correct: The Anti-Kickback Statute (42 USC 1320a-7b) prohibits knowingly and willfully offering, paying, soliciting, or receiving remuneration to induce or reward referrals of items or services reimbursable by federal healthcare programs. The critical element is proving that one purpose of the remuneration was to induce or reward referrals. The statute applies when even one purpose is to generate referrals, not necessarily the sole purpose. The absence of actual consulting work combined with ongoing referrals strongly indicates prohibited intent.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B describes evidence supporting a violation but is not the statutory element itself; payments can violate the AKS even if at fair market value if made to induce referrals. Option C is incorrect because the AKS applies to those in positions to make or influence referrals, not only those with comprehensive patient care authority. Option D is irrelevant to establishing the current violation; prior violations of the payor may be aggravating factors but are not elements of proving the current kickback scheme.

questions.text: A CFE is investigating a physician who owns a 40% interest in an MRI imaging center and refers Medicare patients exclusively to that facility. The physician's referrals generated $890,000 in Medicare billings last year. The physician claims the arrangement fits a Stark Law exception. What should the CFE examine first to determine whether a Stark Law violation exists?
options: A. Whether the physician's ownership and referral arrangement meets all requirements of a specific statutory or regulatory exception to Stark Law
B. Whether the physician disclosed the financial relationship verbally to patients before making referrals to the imaging center
C. Whether the imaging center's fees are below the regional average for comparable MRI services in the geographic market
D. Whether other physicians in the community also refer patients to the same imaging center for diagnostic imaging
questions.justification_correct: The Stark Law (42 USC 1395nn) prohibits physicians from referring Medicare/Medicaid patients for designated health services (DHS) to entities with which they have a financial relationship, unless a specific exception applies. Stark Law is a strict liability statute; intent is irrelevant. The CFE must determine whether all elements of a statutory exception are met (such as in-office ancillary services, fair market value compensation, or other specific exceptions). If no exception applies completely, the referrals violate Stark Law regardless of other factors.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B is incorrect because Stark Law does not contain a patient disclosure exception; verbal disclosure does not cure a prohibited financial relationship. Option C is wrong because below-market pricing does not create a Stark exception; pricing is relevant to fair market value exceptions but does not independently exempt the arrangement. Option D is irrelevant because other physicians' referral patterns do not affect whether this physician's financial relationship violates Stark Law.

questions.text: During a fraud examination, a CFE finds that a physical therapy clinic submitted claims to Medicare for 1,847 therapy sessions over six months. However, appointment logs show only 1,103 patient visits during that period, and patient interviews confirm they did not receive services on many billed dates. What type of fraud scheme is the CFE most likely investigating?
options: A. Billing for services not rendered, evidenced by claims submitted for sessions that did not occur according to clinic records and patient statements
B. Upcoding of therapy services, where lower-intensity treatments were billed as higher-complexity rehabilitation sessions
C. Unbundling of therapy procedures, where comprehensive treatment sessions were split into multiple separately billable components
D. Duplicate billing errors, where the same legitimate therapy sessions were inadvertently submitted multiple times to Medicare
questions.justification_correct: The evidence clearly indicates billing for services not rendered: claims submitted (1,847) exceed documented appointments (1,103), and patients confirm they did not receive services on billed dates. This is one of the most straightforward healthcare fraud schemes, where providers bill for services that never occurred. The discrepancy between claims and both clinic records and patient testimony establishes the fraudulent billing pattern, creating False Claims Act liability.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B (upcoding) involves billing higher-level codes for services that were actually provided at a lower level; here, services were not provided at all. Option C (unbundling) involves separating bundled services into individual billable codes; the issue here is nonexistent services, not improper coding. Option D (duplicate billing) might explain some discrepancy but does not account for patient statements confirming they did not receive services on the dates billed, indicating intentional fabrication rather than administrative error.

questions.text: A CFE has gathered evidence that a durable medical equipment (DME) supplier billed Medicare $2.3 million for power wheelchairs prescribed by physicians who never examined the patients. Delivery records are fabricated, and beneficiaries report never receiving equipment. The CFE is preparing a referral to the Office of Inspector General (OIG). Which documentation is most essential to include?
options: A. Beneficiary statements confirming non-receipt of equipment, billing records showing claims submitted, and evidence that prescribing physicians had no patient contact
B. A comprehensive financial analysis showing the DME supplier's profit margins and comparison to industry standards for similar equipment
C. Background investigations of all company executives including credit histories, prior employment, and professional licensure status
D. Expert opinions from medical device manufacturers regarding the appropriate clinical uses and pricing of power wheelchairs
questions.justification_correct: OIG referrals for DME fraud require documentation establishing the core elements of the fraud: that claims were submitted (billing records), that services/equipment were not provided (beneficiary statements), and that medical necessity documentation was fraudulent (physicians had no patient contact to justify prescriptions). This evidence package demonstrates billing for services not rendered and lack of medical necessity, which are prosecutable under the False Claims Act and can result in civil and criminal penalties. These three elements form the foundation of the fraud scheme.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B, while potentially relevant to damages, does not establish the fraud itself; profit margins are not essential to proving DME fraud. Option C provides background information that may support prosecution but is not essential evidence of the fraud scheme itself; OIG investigators can develop this information. Option D regarding appropriate clinical uses is unnecessary when the fundamental issue is that equipment was never delivered and prescriptions were fraudulent; medical necessity analysis is secondary to proving non-delivery.

questions.text: A CFE has completed an investigation showing that a home health agency knowingly submitted 3,420 false claims to Medicare over four years, totaling $4.8 million in fraudulent billings. The agency received payment on all claims. The U.S. Attorney is considering a civil False Claims Act case. What is the potential civil penalty range under the False Claims Act, excluding treble damages?
options: A. Between $13,946 and $27,894 per false claim, adjusted for inflation, which can total millions in civil penalties alone
B. A maximum of three times the actual damages sustained by the government, with no additional per-claim penalties
C. A flat 15% penalty calculated on the total amount of fraudulent billings submitted to the Medicare program
D. Between $5,000 and $10,000 per false claim as originally enacted, without any adjustments for inflation
questions.justification_correct: The False Claims Act (31 USC 3729) imposes civil penalties per false claim submitted, with amounts adjusted regularly for inflation. As of 2024-2026, penalties range from $13,946 to $27,894 per false claim. These per-claim penalties are in addition to treble (three times) damages. With 3,420 false claims, penalties alone could range from approximately $47.7 million to $95.4 million, plus treble damages of $14.4 million (3 × $4.8 million). The per-claim penalty structure makes the FCA particularly powerful in healthcare fraud prosecutions involving numerous claims.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B incorrectly describes only the treble damages component and omits the mandatory per-claim civil penalties that are central to FCA enforcement. Option C is fictional; the FCA does not use a percentage-based penalty structure. Option D cites outdated penalty amounts; the original $5,000-$10,000 range has been increased through multiple inflation adjustments as required by the Federal Civil Penalties Inflation Adjustment Act, making these figures legally incorrect for current cases.

questions.text: A CFE analyzing hospital billing data notices that claims for CPT codes 26010 (drainage of finger abscess, simple) and 26011 (drainage of finger abscess, complicated) are frequently billed together for the same patient on the same date. Industry standards indicate these represent different complexity levels of the same procedure. Medicare's Correct Coding Initiative (CCI) bundles these codes. What fraud scheme is indicated?
options: A. Unbundling fraud, where components of a single procedure are improperly billed separately to increase reimbursement
B. Upcoding fraud, where simple procedures are systematically billed as more complex procedures to obtain higher payments
C. Billing for services not rendered, where one of the two procedures billed was not actually performed on the patient
D. Duplicate billing, where the same procedure is inadvertently submitted twice using different code numbers
questions.justification_correct: Unbundling (also called fragmentation) occurs when providers bill separately for components that should be billed as a single comprehensive procedure. When codes 26010 and 26011 represent different complexity levels of the same procedure and are billed together for the same encounter, this violates Medicare's Correct Coding Initiative edits. The provider should bill only the appropriate complexity level code, not both. Unbundling increases reimbursement improperly because payers expect comprehensive procedures to be billed with a single code that reflects the complete service.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B (upcoding) would involve billing only code 26011 when only 26010 was performed; here both codes are billed together. Option C assumes one procedure wasn't performed, but the scenario indicates both codes are for the same procedure at different complexity levels, not two separate procedures. Option D (duplicate billing) would involve submitting the same code twice; here different codes are used, and the pattern is systematic rather than inadvertent, indicating intentional unbundling rather than billing error.

questions.text: A CFE investigating a hospital pharmacy discovers that a pharmacy technician with access to controlled substances has been creating false patient records to obtain oxycodone. The technician bills legitimate patient accounts for medications, dispenses them, but then diverts the drugs for personal use or resale. What should the CFE prioritize in documenting this prescription drug diversion scheme?
options: A. Correlate pharmacy dispensing records with actual patient administration records and inventory reconciliations to identify discrepancies indicating diversion
B. Conduct immediate interviews with all pharmacy staff to determine who had knowledge of the diversion scheme and their level of involvement
C. Calculate the street value of diverted medications to establish the financial magnitude of the fraud for prosecution purposes
D. Review the technician's personal financial records and bank statements to trace proceeds from sale of diverted medications
questions.justification_correct: Documenting prescription drug diversion requires establishing that controlled substances were dispensed according to records but never reached patients. The CFE must compare pharmacy dispensing logs (showing drugs were issued to specific patients) against medication administration records (showing what patients actually received) and inventory controls. Discrepancies between these records prove diversion. This documentary evidence establishes the diversion scheme, demonstrates the method used, quantifies the volume of drugs diverted, and creates a foundation for both criminal prosecution and administrative sanctions including DEA violations.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B, while eventually necessary, should not be prioritized before securing documentary evidence; premature interviews can alert subjects and lead to evidence destruction. Option C (street value calculation) is relevant for sentencing and impact but does not establish the diversion scheme itself; proving diversion occurred is the priority. Option D (financial records) may support prosecution by showing the technician's financial benefit, but is secondary to proving the actual diversion through pharmacy records; financial evidence is circumstantial whereas dispensing record discrepancies directly prove diversion.

questions.text: A CFE is using data analytics to detect potential phantom billing in Medicare claims submitted by multiple outpatient clinics. The analysis should identify services billed but likely never provided. Which data pattern would most strongly indicate phantom billing requiring further investigation?
options: A. Clinics billing for services on dates when the facility was documented as closed, or for patients who were hospitalized elsewhere on the claimed service dates
B. Clinics showing higher-than-average billing rates for complex procedures compared to regional benchmarks for similar facilities
C. Variations in billing amounts for the same procedure codes across different clinics within the same geographic region
D. Clinics with high patient volumes during certain months and lower volumes during other months showing seasonal patterns
questions.justification_correct: Phantom billing involves billing for services that could not have occurred. Data showing claims submitted when facilities were closed or patients were elsewhere creates a logical impossibility proving services were not rendered. This pattern provides strong prima facie evidence of fraud because the claims are physically impossible: services cannot be provided in closed facilities, and patients cannot receive outpatient clinic services while hospitalized elsewhere. This analytical approach cross-references billing data with facility operational data and patient location data to identify impossible billing scenarios requiring investigation.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B describes potential upcoding rather than phantom billing; higher rates for complex procedures suggest coding at inappropriate levels for services that were provided. Option C describes normal market variation in pricing and does not indicate phantom billing; different reimbursement rates or billing practices may be legitimate. Option D describes normal business patterns; seasonal variations in patient volume are common and legitimate in healthcare and do not indicate phantom billing or fraud.

questions.text: A CFE discovers that a medical clinic has billed Medicare for services allegedly provided to 127 beneficiaries, but investigation reveals these individuals never visited the clinic and many were unaware their Medicare numbers were used. Clinic employees recruited beneficiaries and paid them $50 to use their information. What is this scheme best characterized as, and what should the CFE's next investigative step be?
options: A. Ghost patient fraud; interview beneficiaries to document they did not receive services and were paid for use of their Medicare information
B. Identity theft fraud; immediately refer to law enforcement without conducting beneficiary interviews to preserve criminal investigation integrity
C. Kickback scheme; focus investigation on payments made to beneficiaries as illegal remuneration under Anti-Kickback Statute
D. Billing error fraud; request the clinic voluntarily refund payments and implement corrective action plans through a self-disclosure
questions.justification_correct: This is ghost patient fraud, where providers bill for services to real Medicare beneficiaries who never received services. The scheme involves recruiting beneficiaries and using their legitimate Medicare information to submit fraudulent claims. The CFE's critical next step is obtaining sworn statements from beneficiaries confirming they did not receive services and were paid for their Medicare information. These statements provide direct evidence proving the billing was fraudulent and establish the systematic nature of the scheme. This evidence is essential for False Claims Act prosecution and supports both civil recovery and criminal charges.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B is incorrect because CFE investigations complement rather than compromise criminal investigations; beneficiary interviews are essential evidence that law enforcement will also need, and immediate referral without developing the case weakens prosecution. Option C mischaracterizes the scheme; while payments to beneficiaries may violate other laws, the primary fraud is billing for non-existent services (ghost patients), not the Anti-Kickback Statute which addresses inducements for legitimate referrals. Option D is inappropriate because the evidence shows intentional, systematic fraud recruitment, not billing errors; voluntary refund programs are for inadvertent errors, not deliberate schemes involving beneficiary recruitment.

questions.text: A CFE is analyzing Medicare Part B claims data for a group of 12 cardiologists in a metropolitan area. Over six months, one cardiologist billed for 1,847 echocardiograms, while the other 11 averaged 412 echocardiograms each. The outlier physician also has a higher-than-average percentage of complex evaluation codes (99215) at 78% versus the peer average of 31%. Which analytical method is most appropriate for the CFE to apply next?
options: A. Compare the physician's billing patterns against national and regional benchmarks for the specialty, and analyze day-by-day billing to identify potential statistical impossibilities
B. Immediately refer the case to the Office of Inspector General without further analysis since the variance exceeds normal statistical deviation
C. Request patient medical records for all 1,847 echocardiograms to verify medical necessity before conducting any statistical comparison
D. Interview the physician directly to obtain an explanation for the variance before performing additional data analysis or peer comparisons
questions.justification_correct: Proper fraud detection methodology requires comparing billing patterns against established benchmarks (both national and regional) and analyzing for statistical impossibilities such as billing for more procedures than physically possible in a day. This data-driven approach provides objective evidence before moving to more resource-intensive steps like medical record review. The significant variance in both volume (4.5x peers) and coding complexity (2.5x peers) suggests potential upcoding and billing for services not rendered, which warrants systematic comparative analysis.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B is premature because CFEs should complete thorough analysis and develop sufficient predication before external referrals; OIG expects preliminary analysis. Option C is inefficient because reviewing 1,847 records without first establishing statistical impossibilities or sampling methodology wastes resources; targeted sampling based on analytical findings is more appropriate. Option D violates investigative protocol because interviewing subjects before completing objective analysis can compromise the investigation, alert the subject, and allow evidence destruction.

questions.text: A hospital system entered into a Corporate Integrity Agreement (CIA) with the Office of Inspector General after settling False Claims Act allegations related to medically unnecessary spinal procedures. The CIA requires annual independent reviews of claims and implementation of a comprehensive compliance program. Eighteen months into the five-year CIA, the CFE serving as compliance officer discovers that an employed neurosurgeon has been systematically upcoding routine spinal injections to more complex procedures. What is the CFE's primary obligation under the CIA?
options: A. Report the discovered overpayments to the appropriate federal healthcare program within 60 days and disclose the matter to OIG as required by the CIA reporting provisions
B. Terminate the neurosurgeon's employment immediately and recover the overpayments through internal restitution to avoid CIA violation
C. Wait until the next scheduled annual independent review to report the findings so that the independent reviewer can verify the compliance issue
D. Conduct a complete internal investigation and self-correct the billing errors without external reporting since this demonstrates effective compliance monitoring
questions.justification_correct: CIAs typically incorporate the 60-day overpayment reporting rule from the Affordable Care Act and contain specific provisions requiring prompt reporting of credible evidence of misconduct to OIG. The CFE's discovery of systematic upcoding triggers both the obligation to report and repay identified overpayments within 60 days and to notify OIG of potential material breaches. Failure to report as required can result in material breach of the CIA, leading to exclusion from federal healthcare programs—a potentially fatal consequence for the hospital. The CIA's reporting requirements supersede internal resolution attempts.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B addresses only part of the obligation; while personnel action may be appropriate, it does not satisfy the mandatory reporting requirements to federal authorities that CIAs impose. Option C is incorrect because CIA reporting obligations are triggered by discovery, not by the scheduled review cycle; waiting could constitute a material breach of the CIA. Option D misunderstands CIA requirements; while self-policing is valued, CIAs explicitly require disclosure of discovered violations to OIG, and failure to report constitutes a separate violation regardless of internal corrective action.

questions.text: A CFE is investigating a Medicaid fraud allegation against a community health center. Analysis of billing records shows that a nurse practitioner billed for 1,240 patient visits over three months for beneficiaries aged 3-17. The CFE's field investigation reveals that 340 of these claimed beneficiary addresses correspond to vacant lots, condemned buildings, or non-existent street numbers. Fourteen beneficiaries share the same date of birth and similar names with minor spelling variations. What fraud scheme is most clearly indicated by these findings?
options: A. Phantom billing using fabricated patient identities and addresses to submit claims for services never rendered to non-existent or ineligible beneficiaries
B. Upcoding of legitimate patient visits combined with duplicate billing under similar patient names to inflate reimbursement amounts
C. Eligibility fraud where the provider is assisting ineligible families in obtaining Medicaid coverage through false address documentation
D. Identity theft where beneficiary information has been stolen and used without the knowledge of real Medicaid-eligible individuals
questions.justification_correct: The evidence pattern strongly indicates phantom billing or ghost patient fraud: claims submitted for patients who do not exist or never received services. The invalid addresses (vacant lots, non-existent numbers) and implausible patient records (identical birthdates with name variations) are hallmarks of fabricated patient rosters. This scheme allows providers to bill for services never rendered by creating fictitious beneficiaries. The systematic nature (340 invalid addresses, 14 duplicate birthdates) indicates intentional fraud rather than data entry errors. This is a common Medicaid fraud scheme because providers exploit the difficulty of verifying beneficiary existence across large populations.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B is incorrect because upcoding involves billing higher-level service codes for actual patients; the invalid addresses indicate the patients themselves are fictitious, not that real services were miscoded. Option C misidentifies the perpetrator and scheme; the evidence shows the provider fabricating patients, not families seeking benefits, as there is no indication real people are involved. Option D is unlikely because mass identity theft would show valid addresses where real people live, not vacant lots and non-existent addresses; this pattern indicates complete fabrication rather than stolen legitimate identities.

questions.text: A CFE is reviewing Medicare Part B claims for a physician-owned clinical laboratory. The data shows that 89% of referred patients receive a comprehensive metabolic panel, lipid panel, CBC, thyroid panel, and vitamin D test regardless of presenting symptoms or diagnosis codes. The referring physicians have investment interests in the laboratory. Many patients with diagnoses such as sprained ankle or seasonal allergies received all five tests. Which primary fraud scheme should the CFE investigate?
options: A. Ordering medically unnecessary tests to generate revenue from which the referring physicians financially benefit through their ownership interests, violating the Stark Law and potentially the Anti-Kickback Statute
B. Unbundling of laboratory services by billing separate test codes that should be reported as a single panel to maximize reimbursement from Medicare
C. Upcoding laboratory test complexity levels by billing for comprehensive panels when only basic metabolic panels were medically necessary for the diagnoses
D. Duplicate billing where the same laboratory tests are being billed multiple times under different test codes for the same patient encounter
questions.justification_correct: The pattern of ordering the same battery of tests regardless of medical necessity, combined with physician ownership interests in the laboratory, indicates violations of both the Stark Law (physician self-referral prohibition) and potentially the Anti-Kickback Statute. Stark Law prohibits physicians from referring Medicare patients for designated health services (including clinical laboratory services) to entities in which they have financial interests. The medical unnecessity—ordering thyroid and lipid panels for ankle sprains—demonstrates that tests are being ordered to generate revenue rather than for clinical need. The physician ownership creates an illegal financial incentive that is the driving force behind the unnecessary utilization pattern.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B describes a different fraud scheme; the issue is not how tests are coded but that they are being ordered without medical necessity due to financial conflicts. The tests described are actually separate valid tests, not components of a single panel being unbundled. Option C is incorrect because the tests were performed as billed; the fraud is ordering them when not medically necessary, not miscoding their complexity. Option D is unsupported by the facts; there is no indication tests are being billed multiple times, only that excessive tests are ordered routinely regardless of diagnosis.

questions.text: A hospital system is establishing a medical directorship arrangement where it will pay cardiologists $5,000 monthly to serve as co-directors of its new cardiovascular service line. The cardiologists will provide administrative oversight, quality review, and strategic planning. They are significant referral sources for cardiac catheterizations and other hospital services. The hospital's CFE is asked to assess whether this arrangement complies with the Anti-Kickback Statute. Which element is most critical to establish safe harbor protection?
options: A. The compensation must be set in advance at fair market value for the actual services provided, determined without considering the volume or value of referrals, and the services must be commercially reasonable and actually needed
B. The arrangement must be disclosed to all patients who are referred by the cardiologists so that patients can make informed decisions about their care
C. The compensation must be less than the aggregate value of the referrals generated by the cardiologists to ensure no net financial benefit from the arrangement
D. The hospital must demonstrate that the cardiologists' referral patterns do not change after entering the arrangement compared to their historical baseline
questions.justification_correct: The personal services and management contracts safe harbor under the Anti-Kickback Statute (42 CFR 1001.952(d)) requires several critical elements: compensation must be set in advance, consistent with fair market value (FMV), not determined in a manner that takes into account the volume or value of referrals, and the services must be commercially reasonable and actually necessary. FMV must be determined through objective means (independent valuation) and must reflect what would be paid in an arm's-length transaction for the same services without regard to referral volume. This protects against disguised payments for referrals. All safe harbor elements must be met for protection; substantial compliance is insufficient.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B is not a requirement of the personal services safe harbor; while transparency may be ethically desirable, patient disclosure is not an element of Anti-Kickback Statute safe harbor protection for compensation arrangements. Option C misstates the standard; fair market value compensation is permitted even if it equals or exceeds referral value, provided it reflects legitimate services and is not calculated based on referrals. The safe harbor does not require compensation to be less than referral value. Option D is not a safe harbor requirement; referral patterns may legitimately change due to quality improvement or clinical integration; the key is that compensation is not determined by or tied to referral volume or value.

questions.text: A CFE is investigating a home health agency that received $4.2 million in Medicare payments over 18 months. Review of clinical documentation for 85 sampled patients reveals that 62 patients were able to independently leave home for social activities, shopping, or regular medical appointments without supportive assistance. Physician certifications indicate homebound status, but therapy notes describe patients as 'ambulating independently in community' and 'driving to outpatient appointments.' What is the primary basis for potential False Claims Act liability?
options: A. Medicare home health services require patients to be homebound, and billing for non-homebound patients constitutes false claims because a condition of payment was not met
B. The therapy notes contain contradictory information that constitutes healthcare fraud through false documentation regardless of actual homebound status
C. The physicians falsely certified homebound status, making them liable under the False Claims Act while the home health agency lacks knowledge of falsity
D. The patients received medically unnecessary services because non-homebound patients do not require skilled nursing or therapy services in the home setting
questions.justification_correct: Medicare home health coverage requires beneficiaries to be 'homebound' as a condition of payment—generally meaning that leaving home requires considerable and taxing effort, and absences are infrequent and of short duration. This is a mandatory condition of coverage under 42 CFR 424.22. When providers bill Medicare for home health services for patients who do not meet the homebound requirement, they submit false claims for services that are not covered. The False Claims Act applies because the provider is claiming payment for services that fail to meet statutory coverage criteria. The agency's own documentation (therapy notes) contradicts the homebound certifications, demonstrating knowledge or reckless disregard of falsity.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B overstates the issue; while contradictory documentation is evidence of fraud, the legal basis for False Claims Act liability is the violation of the homebound coverage requirement, not merely documentation inconsistency. Option C is incorrect because the home health agency can be liable even if physicians also participated; the agency submits the claims and is responsible for ensuring coverage criteria are met; it cannot shield itself by relying on physician certifications when its own records contradict homebound status. Option D conflates medical necessity with coverage criteria; non-homebound patients might benefit from therapy services, but Medicare home health specifically requires homebound status regardless of whether services might be beneficial.

questions.text: A CFE analyzing Medicare Part B claims for an ambulance company discovers that 73% of transports are billed using ALS-1 Emergency (advanced life support) codes, while regional peers average 28% ALS-1 Emergency. The company predominantly transports dialysis patients and nursing home residents to routine appointments. Chart review of 50 transports coded as ALS-1 Emergency shows documentation of stable vital signs, routine transports, and no emergency interventions. What fraud scheme is most clearly indicated?
options: A. Upcoding routine non-emergency transports to emergency advanced life support codes to obtain higher reimbursement rates from Medicare
B. Billing for medically unnecessary ambulance transports when patients could have used alternative transportation methods
C. Unbundling ambulance services by billing separately for basic life support and advanced interventions provided during the same transport
D. Violating the Anti-Kickback Statute by providing free or below-cost transportation to dialysis facilities in exchange for patient referrals
questions.justification_correct: The evidence clearly indicates upcoding: billing higher-level service codes (ALS-1 Emergency) for services that should be billed at lower levels (BLS non-emergency or scheduled). Medicare pays significantly more for emergency ALS transports than routine basic life support transports. ALS-1 Emergency codes require assessment and treatment by ALS personnel and emergency response, not merely transport of patients who happen to require ALS-level capability. The clinical documentation showing stable vital signs, routine scheduled transports, and absence of emergency interventions directly contradicts the ALS-1 Emergency billing. The dramatic variance from peer norms (73% vs. 28%) combined with the population served (dialysis, nursing home) indicates systematic upcoding of routine transports.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B addresses medical necessity for any ambulance transport, but the facts indicate the transports occurred and may have been medically necessary; the fraud is in the level of service billed, not whether ambulance transport was needed. Option C describes unbundling, but there is no indication of separate billing for components; the issue is billing a higher level code for the entire transport. Option D addresses a different fraud scheme; while ambulance companies can violate the Anti-Kickback Statute through referral arrangements, the facts presented indicate a billing fraud (upcoding) rather than an illegal remuneration scheme.

questions.text: A hospital discovers that its billing department has been submitting Medicare claims without reporting known credit balances from prior overpayments. The hospital identified $380,000 in overpayments fourteen months ago but continued submitting new claims without offsetting or reporting these amounts, effectively retaining money it knew it was not entitled to. The CFE is assessing potential False Claims Act liability. Which theory of liability most directly applies?
options: A. Reverse false claims liability under 31 USC 3729(a)(1)(G) for knowingly concealing or avoiding an obligation to pay or transmit money to the government
B. Traditional false claims liability under 31 USC 3729(a)(1)(A) for knowingly presenting false claims because the new claims were tainted by the unreported credit balance
C. Conspiracy to defraud the government under 31 USC 3729(a)(1)(C) because multiple billing department employees were aware of the overpayments
D. Causing false claims to be presented under 31 USC 3729(a)(1)(B) because the billing software caused false claims to be submitted to Medicare
questions.justification_correct: The reverse false claims provision of the False Claims Act (31 USC 3729(a)(1)(G)) creates liability for knowingly concealing or knowingly and improperly avoiding or decreasing an obligation to pay or transmit money or property to the government. The 60-day overpayment rule (42 USC 1320a-7k(d)) creates an obligation to report and return identified overpayments within 60 days. By identifying overpayments but failing to report and return them, the hospital knowingly retained money it was obligated to return. After the 60-day deadline, retained overpayments become 'obligations' under the False Claims Act. This theory has been used successfully in cases involving retention of known overpayments.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B does not fit the facts because the new claims themselves may be legitimate claims for services actually provided; the fraud is in the retention of prior overpayments, not in the submission of new claims. Option C misapplies the conspiracy provision, which requires an agreement to get false claims paid or approved; awareness of overpayments among staff does not constitute conspiracy, and the primary violation here is retention, not conspiracy. Option D is inapplicable because there is no indication the billing software caused false claims; the claims may have been accurate, and the issue is the separate obligation to report and return known overpayments, not a problem with the claims themselves.

questions.text: A CFE is investigating allegations that a pharmaceutical manufacturer provided free practice management software and electronic health records systems valued at $40,000 per physician to oncologists who prescribe the company's chemotherapy drugs. The software includes treatment protocols that default to the manufacturer's products. The manufacturer characterizes this as an educational grant program. Which statute is most directly violated by this arrangement?
options: A. The Anti-Kickback Statute (42 USC 1320a-7b) because providing valuable items to physicians who prescribe federally reimbursed drugs constitutes illegal remuneration intended to induce referrals or purchases
B. The Stark Law because the physicians have a financial relationship with the manufacturer and are referring patients for designated health services
C. The False Claims Act because the software causes physicians to submit false claims to Medicare for chemotherapy drugs that are medically unnecessary
D. The Food Drug and Cosmetic Act because pharmaceutical manufacturers are prohibited from marketing prescription drugs directly to physicians
questions.justification_correct: The Anti-Kickback Statute (42 USC 1320a-7b(b)) prohibits knowingly and willfully offering or paying any remuneration to induce referrals or purchases of items or services covered by federal healthcare programs. 'Remuneration' includes anything of value, including free goods or services such as practice management software. Providing $40,000 in free software to physicians who prescribe the company's drugs constitutes remuneration. The intent to induce prescribing is evidenced by targeting prescribers and incorporating protocols that default to the manufacturer's products. This is a classic pharmaceutical kickback scheme. The characterization as an 'educational grant' is legally insufficient; the substance of the arrangement controls, and providing valuable items to influence prescribing violates the Anti-Kickback Statute.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B is incorrect because the Stark Law applies to physician self-referral for designated health services (like imaging, lab, DME), not to pharmaceutical prescribing; physicians prescribing medications are not making 'referrals' within the meaning of Stark Law. Option C incorrectly identifies the primary violation; while Anti-Kickback Statute violations can provide the basis for False Claims Act liability (false certification theory), the direct violation is the kickback itself, and there is no evidence the drugs are medically unnecessary. Option D misstates the law; pharmaceutical manufacturers may legally market to physicians subject to FDA regulations; the violation here is providing items of value as inducements (kickbacks), not lawful marketing or detailing activities.

questions.text: A CFE is analyzing claims data for dual-eligible beneficiaries (Medicare and Medicaid). The investigation reveals that a medical equipment supplier billed Medicare for power wheelchairs and simultaneously billed Medicaid for the patient cost-sharing amounts (deductibles and coinsurance). For the same patients, Medicaid records show full Medicaid coverage with no patient cost-sharing obligations. The supplier collected $340,000 from Medicaid for cost-sharing that patients never owed. What fraud scheme is indicated?
options: A. Coordination of benefits fraud by billing Medicaid for cost-sharing obligations that do not exist because Medicaid is the payer of last resort and covers cost-sharing for dual-eligible beneficiaries
B. Duplicate billing fraud by submitting claims to both Medicare and Medicaid for the same durable medical equipment without proper coordination
C. Unbundling fraud by billing separately for components that should be included in the Medicare wheelchair reimbursement rate
D. Beneficiary inducement fraud by waiving patient cost-sharing obligations in violation of the Anti-Kickback Statute to attract Medicare patients
questions.justification_correct: This is coordination of benefits (COB) fraud specific to dual-eligible beneficiaries. For individuals with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual-eligibles), Medicaid typically covers Medicare cost-sharing amounts (deductibles, coinsurance) when Medicaid is the secondary payer. Crucially, when Medicaid covers these amounts, there is no patient obligation—the beneficiary owes nothing. By billing Medicaid for cost-sharing amounts for patients whose Medicaid coverage includes cost-sharing, the supplier obtained payment for amounts that were never owed. This constitutes false claims to Medicaid because the supplier certified there was a patient obligation when none existed. The $340,000 represents payments for non-existent cost-sharing obligations.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B is incorrect because billing both programs is legitimate for dual-eligibles when properly coordinated (Medicare primary, Medicaid secondary for cost-sharing); the fraud is in billing for cost-sharing that patients did not owe, not in billing both programs. Option C does not fit the facts; there is no indication that wheelchair components were unbundled, and the fraud involves the cost-sharing billing to Medicaid, not the equipment billing structure. Option D reverses the situation; the supplier did not waive cost-sharing (which could violate the Anti-Kickback Statute in some contexts), but rather fraudulently billed Medicaid for cost-sharing that the beneficiaries never owed because their Medicaid coverage included cost-sharing.

questions.text: A CFE is engaged by a healthcare system to prioritize fraud risk areas for the upcoming year. The compliance director provides a copy of the current Office of Inspector General (OIG) Work Plan and asks the CFE to identify which areas warrant immediate internal audit attention based on OIG enforcement priorities. What should the CFE do first?
options: A. Review the Work Plan to identify areas where the healthcare system has operations matching OIG's stated audit and investigation priorities for the current fiscal year
B. Conduct interviews with all department heads to determine which areas they believe present the highest compliance risk regardless of OIG priorities
C. Immediately implement data analytics on all billing codes to identify statistical outliers across the entire revenue cycle
D. Request historical OIG settlement agreements involving similar healthcare systems to determine which fraud schemes resulted in the largest penalties
questions.justification_correct: The OIG Work Plan is published annually and identifies specific areas the OIG intends to audit and investigate, representing current enforcement priorities. A CFE should first compare the organization's operations against these stated priorities to identify relevant high-risk areas. This targeted approach allows efficient allocation of compliance resources to areas under active federal scrutiny, making it the most strategic first step in risk-based audit planning.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B bypasses the valuable intelligence provided by the OIG Work Plan, which reflects actual enforcement priorities rather than subjective internal perceptions. Option C is premature and inefficient without first identifying which specific areas warrant scrutiny based on OIG priorities. Option D focuses on historical penalties rather than current enforcement priorities outlined in the Work Plan, and settlement amounts do not necessarily correlate with current OIG focus areas.

questions.text: A CFE is investigating a psychiatrist who billed Medicare for 18 individual psychotherapy sessions per day, every weekday for six months. Medical records show appointments scheduled in 30-minute blocks from 8:00 AM to 6:00 PM with minimal documentation. Several patients, when interviewed, report they participated in group therapy sessions, not individual sessions. What fraud scheme is most likely occurring?
options: A. Upcoding by billing individual psychotherapy codes (90834) when group psychotherapy (90853) was actually provided to maximize reimbursement
B. Phantom billing for services not rendered to ghost patients who were never seen by the psychiatrist
C. Unbundling by separately billing psychotherapy and evaluation codes that should be reported together under a single comprehensive code
D. Billing medically unnecessary services by providing psychotherapy to patients who did not meet clinical criteria for treatment
questions.justification_correct: The evidence indicates the psychiatrist conducted group therapy sessions but billed them as higher-reimbursing individual psychotherapy sessions. Individual psychotherapy codes (such as 90834) pay significantly more than group psychotherapy codes (such as 90853). Patient interviews confirming group participation, combined with the scheduling pattern and minimal documentation, strongly indicate upcoding—billing a higher-level service code than what was actually provided. This is a common mental health fraud scheme because the reimbursement differential is substantial.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B is incorrect because patients confirmed they actually attended sessions, eliminating phantom billing. Option C is incorrect because there is no indication of separate billing for services that should be bundled; the issue is misrepresenting the type of service (group vs. individual). Option D is incorrect because there is no evidence regarding whether patients met clinical criteria; the fraud involves misrepresenting the type of service provided, not its necessity.

questions.text: During enrollment verification for new Medicare providers, a CFE discovers that a physician submitted an application claiming board certification in cardiology and listing hospital privileges at three facilities. When the CFE contacts the American Board of Internal Cardiology, no record exists for this physician. The National Practitioner Data Bank shows two malpractice judgments under a similar name with a different middle initial. What should the CFE do next?
options: A. Verify the physician's medical license with the state medical board, confirm identity through primary source verification, and check whether the hospital privileges are authentic
B. Immediately notify law enforcement and recommend denial of the Medicare enrollment application based on the false board certification claim
C. Assume the similar name in the National Practitioner Data Bank refers to a different individual and proceed with enrollment pending board certification clarification
D. Contact the three hospitals to determine if the physician has admitting privileges and use that confirmation as sufficient verification of credentials
questions.justification_correct: Primary source verification is the standard for credentialing investigations. The CFE must systematically verify credentials directly with authoritative sources: the state medical board for licensure, the hospitals for privileges, and resolve the identity question given the National Practitioner Data Bank findings. Multiple red flags (false board certification, name discrepancies, malpractice history) require thorough verification before making recommendations. This methodical approach ensures accurate identification and prevents both enrollment of fraudulent providers and mistaken denial of legitimate applicants.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B is premature without completing verification; while the false certification is serious, the CFE must confirm identity and other credentials before making final recommendations. Option C is negligent because name variations with malpractice history require definitive identity verification, especially combined with the false board certification claim. Option D is insufficient because hospital privileges alone do not verify medical licensure, board certification, or identity, and hospitals may have been deceived by the same fraudulent credentials.

questions.text: A CFE reviewing medical records for a Medicare fraud investigation finds that an orthopedic surgeon performed and billed for 45 knee arthroscopy procedures in a three-month period. Documentation for each procedure states 'knee pain' as the indication, with operative notes showing routine diagnostic arthroscopy with no significant findings or therapeutic intervention in 40 of the 45 cases. What fraud element should the CFE focus on?
options: A. Medical necessity, because Medicare only covers services that are reasonable and necessary for diagnosis or treatment, and diagnostic arthroscopy without therapeutic purpose may not meet this standard
B. Upcoding, because the surgeon likely billed for therapeutic arthroscopy procedures when only diagnostic examinations were performed
C. Unbundling, because the surgeon should have billed a comprehensive code that includes both the diagnostic and therapeutic components together
D. Kickback violations, because the high volume of unnecessary procedures suggests the surgeon received illegal remuneration for patient referrals
questions.justification_correct: Medical necessity is a fundamental Medicare coverage requirement under 42 USC 1395y(a)(1)(A). Services must be reasonable and necessary for diagnosis or treatment of illness or injury. Performing invasive diagnostic arthroscopy when less invasive diagnostic methods (MRI, X-ray, physical examination) would suffice, and finding nothing significant in 89% of cases, suggests procedures that may not meet the medical necessity standard. The pattern of routine procedures with minimal findings and no therapeutic benefit is a classic medical necessity fraud indicator.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B is incorrect because the operative notes accurately describe what was done (diagnostic arthroscopy); there is no evidence the surgeon billed for more complex therapeutic procedures than performed. Option C is incorrect because there is no indication of separate billing for components that should be bundled; the issue is whether the procedures should have been performed at all. Option D is unsupported by the facts; high volume of potentially unnecessary procedures alone does not indicate kickbacks, which require evidence of illegal remuneration for referrals.

questions.text: A CFE is investigating a hospice provider whose Medicare billing increased 300% over two years. Review of medical records for 50 patients reveals that 32 patients had cancer diagnoses but recent imaging showing stable or improved conditions, 18 patients remained on service for over two years, and 12 patients were discharged to hospitals for curative treatment then re-enrolled in hospice. What is the primary fraud scheme indicated?
options: A. Billing for patients who do not meet hospice eligibility requirements, specifically the certification that patients have a life expectancy of six months or less if the disease runs its normal course
B. Phantom billing by claiming services for patients who were not actually receiving hospice care at the facilities listed on the claims
C. Upcoding by billing higher levels of hospice care than were medically necessary based on patient acuity and service intensity
D. Unbundling by separately billing for services that should be included in the comprehensive hospice per diem rate
questions.justification_correct: Hospice Medicare benefit requires certification that patients have a terminal illness with life expectancy of six months or less if the disease runs its normal course (42 CFR 418.22). The evidence shows systematic enrollment of ineligible patients: those with stable/improving conditions, extremely long lengths of stay (over 2 years), and patients seeking curative treatment (contradicting hospice's palliative focus). These patterns indicate the hospice is enrolling and billing for patients who do not meet the fundamental eligibility criterion, which is the core fraud scheme driving the billing increase.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B is incorrect because there is no evidence patients were non-existent or not receiving services; the issue is their eligibility for hospice, not whether services occurred. Option C is incorrect because hospice has four levels of care, but the evidence does not suggest billing for higher acuity levels than provided; rather, it indicates patients should not be in hospice at all. Option D is incorrect because the issue is not improper separate billing for included services, but rather billing for ineligible patients.

questions.text: Following the COVID-19 public health emergency, a CFE is reviewing telemedicine billing by a multi-state medical group. Analysis reveals that a physician billed Medicare for 85 telemedicine visits in a single day, with session times documented as 45-60 minutes each. Video platform logs show total login time of 6.5 hours that day, with individual session durations averaging 8-12 minutes. What fraud scheme is most evident?
options: A. Upcoding by billing for higher-level evaluation and management codes requiring longer time or complexity than the actual telemedicine encounters warranted
B. Unbundling by billing telemedicine visits and remote monitoring services separately when they should be reported under a single code
C. Billing for services not rendered because the physician could not have provided 85 sessions of 45-60 minutes in a 6.5-hour period
D. Stark Law violations by billing for telemedicine services provided to patients referred by physicians who have financial relationships with the medical group
questions.justification_correct: Time-based evaluation and management (E/M) coding requires that the documented time reasonably reflects the actual encounter duration. The physician documented 45-60 minutes per visit (suggesting codes like 99215 or 99205), but platform logs prove sessions averaged only 8-12 minutes. This is upcoding—billing higher-level codes than warranted by the service provided. The mathematical impossibility (85 × 45 minutes = 3,825 minutes vs. 390 minutes actual) combined with platform verification makes this clear upcoding rather than complete fabrication of services.
questions.justification_wrong: Option C is technically imprecise because services were actually rendered (platform logs confirm 6.5 hours of sessions); the fraud is misrepresenting their level/duration, not complete fabrication. While severe, this is upcoding rather than phantom billing. Option B is unsupported because there is no evidence of separate billing for services that should be bundled. Option D is unsupported by the facts; there is no information about physician financial relationships or referral patterns, and the evidence clearly points to upcoding based on time documentation fraud.

questions.text: A CFE is conducting compliance due diligence for a hospital acquiring a physician practice. During vendor screening, the CFE discovers that one of the practice's part-time physicians appears on the OIG List of Excluded Individuals and Entities (LEIE) due to a conviction three years ago. The practice has been billing Medicare for services provided by this physician. What are the implications?
options: A. The practice has submitted false claims to Medicare because excluded individuals cannot participate in federal healthcare programs, and any claims for their services are not payable, potentially triggering False Claims Act liability

B. The exclusion only prevents the individual physician from directly billing Medicare but does not affect the practice's ability to bill for services the physician provided under the practice's group NPI

C. The hospital can cure the violation by immediately terminating the physician and implementing prospective screening, with no liability for past claims if termination occurs before acquisition closes
D. The practice must refund only the professional component of claims while retaining the facility fees because the services were actually rendered to patients
questions.justification_correct: Under 42 USC 1320a-7(a), excluded individuals cannot participate in federal healthcare programs in any capacity. No federal healthcare program payment may be made for any item or service furnished by an excluded individual, regardless of whether billed under the individual's NPI or the group's NPI. Billing for services provided by excluded individuals constitutes submission of false claims, exposing the practice to False Claims Act liability (treble damages plus penalties per claim). This is why monthly LEIE screening is a critical compliance function.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B misunderstands exclusion scope; excluded individuals cannot participate in any capacity, and billing under a group NPI does not circumvent the prohibition. Option C is incorrect because False Claims Act liability attaches when false claims were submitted; prospective corrective action does not eliminate past liability, though it may be considered mitigation. The acquiring hospital also faces potential liability. Option D is incorrect because the entire claim (both professional and facility components) is not payable when furnished by an excluded individual; there is no provision for partial payment.

questions.text: A CFE investigating a nursing home finds that the facility billed Medicare Part A for 95% of residents at the highest case-mix classification (RUG category) for skilled nursing, indicating maximum care needs. Staffing records show nurse-to-patient ratios below state minimums, and interviews with discharged patients reveal that many were ambulatory and required minimal assistance. What fraud scheme is most likely present?
options: A. Upcoding by inflating patient acuity classifications to bill higher Resource Utilization Group rates than justified by actual care needs and services provided
B. Billing for services not rendered because the inadequate staffing levels made it impossible to provide the intensive services that were billed
C. Kickback violations because the high billing rates suggest the facility paid illegal remuneration to hospitals for patient referrals
D. Unbundling by separately billing Medicare for nursing services and ancillary services that should be included in the per diem skilled nursing facility rate
questions.justification_correct: Medicare Part A pays skilled nursing facilities under the RUG (Resource Utilization Group) system, where reimbursement rates are based on patient acuity and care needs assessed through the MDS (Minimum Data Set). When 95% of residents are classified at the highest acuity level despite evidence they were ambulatory with minimal needs, this indicates systematic upcoding—falsifying or exaggerating patient assessments to justify higher RUG categories and reimbursement. This is one of the most common nursing home fraud schemes and a persistent OIG concern.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B, while the understaffing is problematic and may constitute quality of care issues, the primary fraud is the misrepresentation of patient acuity for billing purposes (upcoding), not complete absence of services. Option C is unsupported by the facts; high billing rates alone do not indicate kickbacks, which require evidence of illegal remuneration. The evidence points to RUG manipulation, not referral payments. Option D is incorrect because SNF Part A uses a comprehensive per diem rate; the issue is not unbundling but rather inflating the case-mix classification that determines the per diem rate.

questions.text: A CFE analyzing billing patterns at an outpatient surgical center notices that a gastroenterologist performed colonoscopies on 22 patients in a single day. For 19 of these patients, the claims include both a screening colonoscopy code and a diagnostic colonoscopy code with polypectomy. Chart review shows that biopsies or polyp removals occurred in all 19 cases. What should the CFE investigate?
options: A. Unbundling, because when a screening colonoscopy becomes diagnostic due to findings during the procedure, only the diagnostic code with appropriate modifiers should be billed, not both separately
B. Upcoding, because the physician billed for complex polypectomy procedures when only simple biopsies were performed during routine screenings
C. Billing for medically unnecessary services because performing biopsies or removing polyps in 86% of screening cases suggests the physician is creating false findings
D. Phantom billing, because billing both screening and diagnostic codes for the same encounter indicates duplicate billing for a single procedure
questions.justification_correct: Medicare and other payers have specific rules for colonoscopy coding: when a screening colonoscopy results in findings requiring diagnostic intervention (biopsy, polypectomy), the claim should reflect only the diagnostic procedure code with modifier PT (if applicable) to indicate it began as a screening. Billing both a screening code and a separate diagnostic code for the same colonoscopy session is unbundling—separately billing for components that should be reported under a single comprehensive code. This is a common fraud scheme in endoscopy centers because it results in duplicate payment for a single procedure.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B is incorrect because there is no evidence the procedures were misrepresented as more complex than performed; the issue is billing both screening and diagnostic codes for one procedure. Option C is unfounded; finding polyps or abnormalities in 86% of cases, while high, is not inherently impossible or indicative of fabrication without additional evidence such as pathology report analysis. Option D incorrectly characterizes the scheme; this is unbundling (separating bundled services), not phantom billing (billing for non-existent services or encounters).

questions.text: A hospital compliance officer contacts a CFE because a nurse reported that physicians are routinely billing for procedures that residents actually performed without attending physician presence. The nurse wants to report this to authorities but fears retaliation and termination. What should the CFE advise regarding the nurse's legal protections?
options: A. The nurse is protected under the False Claims Act's anti-retaliation provisions (31 USC 3730(h)), which prohibit discharge or discrimination against employees who report suspected fraud against government healthcare programs
B. The nurse has no legal protection unless she first reports the conduct through internal compliance channels and allows the hospital 90 days to investigate before contacting authorities
C. Whistleblower protections only apply if the nurse files a qui tam lawsuit under seal; informal reports to law enforcement or OIG do not trigger anti-retaliation protections
D. The nurse should consult hospital human resources to determine if the employee handbook provides whistleblower protection, as federal law does not cover internal fraud reporting
questions.justification_correct: The False Claims Act provides robust anti-retaliation protection under 31 USC 3730(h) for employees who engage in lawful acts in furtherance of an FCA action or efforts to stop violations. This protection applies to employees who report suspected fraud involving government programs (including Medicare/Medicaid), whether through qui tam filing, reporting to authorities, or internal reporting. Protected individuals who experience retaliation are entitled to reinstatement, double back pay, and other relief. This is critical protection for healthcare whistleblowers reporting fraud.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B is incorrect because FCA anti-retaliation protection does not require exhaustion of internal remedies; employees are protected for reporting suspected fraud regardless of whether they use internal channels first. Option C is incorrect because anti-retaliation protection extends beyond qui tam relators to any employee who takes lawful action to stop FCA violations, including reporting to government agencies. Option D is incorrect because federal law (FCA, among others) does provide whistleblower protection for reporting healthcare fraud; protection does not depend solely on employer policies.

questions.text: A CFE is investigating a spinal surgeon who holds a 35% ownership stake in a medical device distributorship that sells orthopedic implants. The distributorship purchases implants from manufacturers at standard wholesale prices and sells them to hospitals where the surgeon performs procedures. The surgeon receives distributions proportional to overall company profits, not tied to his individual implant usage. The distributorship also sells to facilities where the surgeon has no privileges. Hospital reimbursement comes from Medicare. Which factor creates the MOST significant Anti-Kickback Statute exposure in this arrangement?
options: A. The surgeon's ownership interest in the distributorship creates a financial incentive to choose facilities based on implant purchasing relationships rather than patient best interest
B. The distributorship's markup over wholesale price generates remuneration that flows back to the physician in proportion to his ownership stake
C. The surgeon receives profit distributions that correlate with the volume of implants sold, even if not explicitly tied to his own procedures
D. The arrangement lacks adequate disclosure to Medicare and patients regarding the surgeon's financial relationship with the implant supplier
questions.justification_correct: Under the Anti-Kickback Statute (42 USC 1320a-7b), the primary concern is that remuneration creates an incentive to make referrals or purchasing decisions based on financial gain rather than patient best interest. The most dangerous element here is that the surgeon's ownership may influence where he performs procedures (facility selection constitutes a referral decision) based on which hospitals purchase from his distributorship. This creates a closed loop where the surgeon steers cases to facilities that buy implants generating his profits, constituting an illegal inducement for referrals. Even with proportional distributions and sales to other facilities, the structure incentivizes referral decisions based on financial return rather than clinical appropriateness.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B identifies the profit flow mechanism but misses the core violation—the AKS prohibits remuneration that induces referrals, not merely profit generation itself. Many lawful business arrangements involve markups and returns to investors. Option C focuses on volume correlation, but the statute's concern is whether remuneration induces referrals, not whether compensation correlates with volume per se (the statute doesn't require explicit per-procedure payments). Option D addresses disclosure, but the AKS is violated by the underlying arrangement that induces referrals regardless of disclosure; transparency doesn't cure an illegal kickback scheme, though it may be relevant to intent and safe harbor analysis.

questions.text: An orthopedic group practice is analyzing potential Stark Law violations involving Dr. Martinez, who refers patients for MRI services. Dr. Martinez has three financial relationships: (1) she owns 8% equity in the group practice, which owns the MRI center as a wholly-owned subsidiary; (2) she receives productivity bonuses calculated on her individual collections, including evaluation and management services; (3) she participates in a profit-sharing plan distributing MRI center net revenues equally among all 12 physician-owners. Last year, her MRI referrals represented 31% of the center's volume. Which relationship creates the GREATEST Stark Law prohibition concern?
options: A. The profit-sharing arrangement that distributes MRI revenues equally, because her compensation varies with the volume of her own referrals to the designated health service
B. The equity ownership in the group practice, because it constitutes an indirect ownership interest in the MRI subsidiary that receives her referrals
C. The productivity bonus based on individual collections, because it creates a direct financial incentive to increase evaluation services that lead to MRI referrals
